coredata.dc:title: Construction of hierarchical porous carbon nanosheets from template-assisted assembly of coal-based graphene quantum dots for high performance supercapacitor electrodes
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Su
Zhu, Jiayao
Qing, Yan
Fan, Chengwei
Wang, Luxiang
Huang, Yudai
Sheng, Rui
Guo, Yong
Wang, Tao
Pan, Yanliang
Lv, Yan
Song, Huaihe
Jia, Dianzeng
coredata.dc:description: Using coal-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the building blocks, we develop a simple template-assisted assembly strategy to prepare hierarchical porous carbon nanosheets (HPCNs) for supercapacitor electrodes. The coal-based GQDs are prepared by a simple liquid phase oxidation of the bituminous coal. Benefiting from their small size, enriched edge structure, abundant functional groups, and good flexibility and chemical reactivity, these GQDs are suitable to construct complex nanoarchitectures for advanced energy storage materials. As a result, the HPCNs show an interconnected loose-stacking graphene-like structure with the specific surface area of 1332 m2 g−1, hierarchical pore distribution, excellent conductivity, abundant active sites, and sufficient ion migration channels. An in situ chemical activation is further applied to improve its energy storage performance. Under the optimized conditions, the activated HPCN with a specific surface area of 1450 m2 g−1 shows a greatly improved capacitive performance, with a high specific capacitance of 230 F g−1 (1 A g−1) and capacitance retention of 74% at 100 A g−1 (170 F g−1). No obvious capacity fade was found even after cycled at 10 A g−1 for 10,000 times, demonstrating their excellent endurability. Our work may provide new thought for the effective use of abundant coal resource to design and preparation advanced carbon nanoarchitectures for energy storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Coal
Carbon nanosheets
Hierarchical porous structure
Supercapacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic description for the preparation of the HPCNs.

(a1–a3) HRTEM images of the coal-based GQDs; (b1–b3) SEM images of the as-prepared Mg(OH)2 template; (c1–c3) SEM, (d1–d3) HRTEM, (e1) STEM images, and the corresponding (e2) C and (e3) O maps of the HPCN-1: 3.
(a) XRD, (b) Raman, (c) FT-IR, and (d) XPS C1s patterns of the as-prepared HPCNs.
(a) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and (b) pore size distributions of the HPCNs.

(a) CV under the scan rate of 10 mV s−1 and (b) galvanostatic charge–discharge curves under 1 A g−1 of the HPCNs prepared with different template addition ratio; (c) CV curves of the HPCN-1: 3 under different scan rates from 10 to 500 mV s−1; (d) the rate performance, (e) the cycle performance under 10 A g−1, and (f) the EIS spectra of the HPCNs prepared with different template addition ratio.
(a1–a3) SEM, (b1–b3) HRTEM, (c1) STEM images and the corresponding (c2) C and (c3) O maps of the AHPCN-1: 0.25. (d1–d3) SEM and (e1–e3) HRTEM images of the AHPCN-1: 1.
(a) XRD, (b) Raman, (c) FT-IR, and (d) XPS C1s patterns of the HPCN-1: 3 and the AHPCNs.
(a) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and (b) pore size distributions of the HPCN-1: 3 and the AHPCNs.

(a) CV under the scan rate of 10 mV s−1 and (b) galvanostatic charge–discharge curves under 1 A g−1 of the HPCN-1: 3 and the AHPCNs; (c) CV curves of the AHPCN-1: 0.25 under different scan rates from 10 to 1000 mV s−1; (d) the rate performance, (e) the cycle performance under 10 A g−1, and (f) the EIS spectra of the HPCN-1: 3 and the AHPCNs.
Related concentration of carbon and oxygen in the HPCNs from survey XPS spectra, and contribution of the component in the area of the XPS C1s spectra.

head.ce:title: Construction of hierarchical porous carbon nanosheets from template-assisted assembly of coal-based graphene quantum dots for high performance supercapacitor electrodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830046, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry
Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering
Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using coal-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the building blocks, we develop a simple template-assisted assembly strategy to prepare hierarchical porous carbon nanosheets (HPCNs) for supercapacitor electrodes. The coal-based GQDs are prepared by a simple liquid phase oxidation of the bituminous coal. Benefiting from their small size, enriched edge structure, abundant functional groups, and good flexibility and chemical reactivity, these GQDs are suitable to construct complex nanoarchitectures for advanced energy storage materials. As a result, the HPCNs show an interconnected loose-stacking graphene-like structure with the specific surface area of 1332 m2 g−1, hierarchical pore distribution, excellent conductivity, abundant active sites, and sufficient ion migration channels. An in situ chemical activation is further applied to improve its energy storage performance. Under the optimized conditions, the activated HPCN with a specific surface area of 1450 m2 g−1 shows a greatly improved capacitive performance, with a high specific capacitance of 230 F g−1 (1 A g−1) and capacitance retention of 74% at 100 A g−1 (170 F g−1). No obvious capacity fade was found even after cycled at 10 A g−1 for 10,000 times, demonstrating their excellent endurability. Our work may provide new thought for the effective use of abundant coal resource to design and preparation advanced carbon nanoarchitectures for energy storage.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are commonly considered as a kind of zero-dimensional graphene derivations with the lamellar size smaller than 10 nm. Due to their size effects and unique physicochemical properties, GQDs have attracted great attention in recent years especially on electronic devices, photoluminescent materials, bio-imaging, bio-sensors, and solar cells [1–4]. When used for supercapacitors or micro-supercapacitors, the GQDs performed good capacitive as well as rate performance due to their large specific surface area, good conductivity, and abundant active sites and accessible edges [5–9]. For example, Yan et al. reported that when used for micro-supercapacitors, the GQDs showed a superior rate capability up to 1000 V s−1 [5]. Recently, several graphene or carbon quantum dot based composites such as GQD coated VO2 nanobelts [10], CuO nanowires [11], GQD-doped polyaniline [12], and carbon quantum dot decorated NiCo2O4 nanoparticles [13,14] were also prepared and applied for energy storage materials.

The GQDs would be promising precursors to prepare advanced energy storage materials because (1) the conjugated carbon skeleton with zero-dimensional structure are much flexible to construct complex and conductive architectures, (2) the enriched edge structure and functional groups may provide large numbers of active sites for energy storage, and (3) the good chemical reactivity and migration property allows their easy assembly or operation. But tedious preparation and separation routes result in the low yields of the GQDs, thus greatly hindered the development of GQD based advanced electrode materials. In this case, GQDs were commonly applied for energy storage by hybridizing with other carbons such as carbon nanotubes [15], graphene nanosheets [16,17], and carbon fibers [18] in the previous works. Zhu et al. developed an ice-template method to assemble fullerene derived GQDs into carbon nanosheets. They showed a high volumetric capacitance of 157.4 F cm−3 when used for supercapacitors [19]. Coal is an abundant mineral resource which have been widely used for chemical industry, energy source, and preparation of carbon products. But with the ever increasing resource and environmental problems, developing new routes for clean and efficient use of coal resource are urgently demanded. Previously, coal has already been used for the preparation of advanced carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes [20,21], graphene nanosheets [22,23] and graphene nanoribbons [24]. Tour et al. presented a novel chemical oxidation approach for the large-scale preparation of the GQDs from cheap and abundant coal, which opens up a new way for the efficient use of coal resource [25–28]. Qiu et al. [29,30] and Chi et al. [31] also prepared some functional coal-based GQDs by chemical oxidation and investigated their optical properties. In our previous work, the high oxidative GQDs were used for solution- or solid-state supercapacitor electrolytes [32].

Template method is a simple and effective way to design and prepare special and complex nanoarchitectures. In the early 2010s, Yang et al. prepared a novel hollow carbon nanospheres constructed by nanographene as the building blocks using core-space-shell silica nanospheres as the template [33]. To some extent, these nanographene could be considered as a kind of GQDs. Fan et al. prepared a novel pillared porous carbon nanosheets by using porous MgO as the template. This material showed a very high capacitance of 289 F g−1 and good rate performance [34,35]. Hu et al. prepared several kinds of thin layer carbon nanocages for high performance supercapacitor electrodes by chemical vapor deposition using MgO nanoparticles as the template [36,37]. Herein, using the coal-based GQDs as the building blocks, we developed a simple template-assisted assembly method to prepare hierarchical porous carbon nanosheets (HPCNs). The HPCNs show an interconnected loose-stacking structure with high specific surface area (up to 1332 m2 g−1), hierarchical pore distribution, and excellent conductivity. Therefore, they perform good high energy storage capability, excellent rate performance, and good endurability as the electrode materials for supercapacitors. To further improve their electrochemical performance, an in situ activation route is applied by the addition of only small amount of KOH. Under the optimized conditions, the activated HPCN with a specific surface area of 1450 m2 g−1 shows a greatly improved capacitive performance, with a high specific capacitance of 230 F g−1 (1 A g−1) and capacitance retention of 74% at 100 A g−1 (170 F g−1). No obvious capacity fade was found even after cycled at 10 A g−1 for 10000 times, demonstrating their excellent endurability.

The schematic for the preparation procedure of the HPCNs is given in Fig. 1 . All of the chemical agents were used as received without further purification. Bituminous coal (Heishan, Xinjiang, China) was used as the precursor. The coal-based GQDs are prepared by a chemical oxidation method according to Ref. 19 but with some modification. It must be noticed that all of the reactions were carried out in a fuming cupboard. The details are given in the Supporting information.

The home-made Mg(OH)2 was used as the template, the preparation method was seen in the Supporting information. For the preparation of the HPCNs, 0.5 g of the coal-based GQDs and a certain amount of the Mg(OH)2 were fully dispersed into 60 mL of the ethanol with the assistance of ultra-sonication and vigorous stirring. After dried at 80 °C, the mixture was carbonized under N2 atmosphere at 800 °C for 1 h with the heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The HPCNs were obtained after fully removal of the template using diluted HCl and washed to neutral by deionized water. The samples were named as HPCN-ratio where the “ratio” stands for the ratio between the GQDs and the Mg(OH)2 template (For example, the HPCN made by 0.5 g of the GQDs and 1.5 g of the Mg(OH)2 was named as HPCN-1: 3).

For the in situ chemical activation, 0.5 g of the coal-based GQDs, 1.5 g of the Mg(OH)2, and a certain amount of the KOH were fully dispersed into 60 mL of the ethanol with the assistance of ultra-sonication and vigorous stirring. The further preparation steps are similar as above mentioned. The sample was named as AHPCN-ratio where the “ratio” stands for the ratio between the GQDs and the KOH (For example, the AHPCN made by 0.5 g of the GQDs and 0.25 g of the KOH was named as AHPCN-1: 0.5).

The morphology and structure characterizations were carried out using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2100F). The scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) and the elemental mapping were measured on a JEM ARM-200F transmission electron microscopy. The crystal structure of the samples were measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD) recorded on a Rigaku D/max-2500B2+/PCX system operating at 40 kV and 20 mA using Cu-Kα radiation. Raman spectroscopy was recorded on a Bruker SENTERRA dispersive Raman spectrometer with 532 nm wave length incident laser light. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a ESCALAB 250 Xi spectrometer using a monochromatic Al-Kα X-ray source (1486.7 eV). The binding energy scale was internally calibrated to the energy 284.4 eV of the C1s peak. The C1s core level spectra were deconvoluted by fitting using symmetric Gaussian/Lorentzian function after subtraction of the background signal by Shirley's method. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was measured by Nicolet Nexus 670 infrared spectroscopy instrument. The samples were characterized by N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms at −196 °C using a volumetric adsorption apparatus ASAP 2020 (Micromeritics, USA). The surface area was obtained by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method. The pore size distribution was calculated by using nitrogen desorption branches via a Non-Local Density Functional Theory (NLDFT) method.

The electrodes using for testing electrochemical properties were prepared as follows. The as-prepared sample, acetylene black (conductive agent), and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE, binding agent) were mixed together with a mass ratio of 85: 10: 5. The mixture was milled to slurry by the addition of small amount of ethanol, and then coated on the nickel foam (ca. 1 × 1 cm). The electrodes were obtained after dried at 110 °C in a vacuum oven and subsequent compacted at ca. 10 MPa. For each of the electrode, the active component should be more than 2 mg. The standard three-electrode system constructed by Platinum electrode (1.5 × 1.5 cm) as the counter electrode, standard Hg/HgO electrode (0.098 V) as the reference electrode, and 6 M KOH aqueous solution as the electrolyte was used to test all the following measurements. The electrolytic tank contains a Luggin capillary to reduce the testing error. The potential window was set between −0.9 and 0.1 V. The galvanostatic charge/discharge tests were carried out on LanHe-CT2001A system under the current density of 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 80, and 100 A g−1. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) was measured at a scan rate of 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, and 500 mV s−1 using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in a frequency range from 1 Hz to 10 kHz with AC amplitude of 5 mV.

Fig. 2 a1–a3 show the HRTEM images of the coal-based GQDs. The diameter of these GQDs is ranging from 2 to 3.2 nm, the statistic size distribution is given in Fig. S1. The FT-IR, XPS C1s , thermal gravimetric, and Raman curves of the GQDs are given in Fig. S2. Initially, the GQDs contain ca. 45 at. % of the oxygen which mainly formed as COOH, CO, and COC (Fig. S2a and b). The high oxidative nature of the GQDs ensures their good dispersibility and chemical reactivity. From the SEM images given in Fig. 2b1–b3, the as-prepared Mg(OH)2 template shows a loose stacking lamellar structure. The HPCNs were obtained by a simple Mg(OH)2 template induced assembly route. The SEM (Fig. 2c1–c3) and the HRTEM (Fig. 2d1–d3) images illustrate that the HPCN-1: 3 is constructed by a large number of loose stacked thin-layer carbon nanosheets. And these carbon nanosheets show an amorphous structure and are interconnected with each other. The HPCN-1: 2 and the HPCN-1: 4 exhibit the similar morphologies as that of the HPCN-1: 3. The SEM and the HRTEM images are given in Fig. S3. From the STEM image and elemental maps given in Fig. 2e1–e3, we can find that there are still some oxygen remained and uniformly distributes in carbon skeleton after carbonization. These results suggest that (1) the coal-based GQDs are successfully transformed into carbon nanosheets by Mg(OH)2 template inducing, (2) the unique two-dimensional feature allows large specific surface area and abundant energy storage sites, (3) the loose stacking structure provides large amount of macro- and meso-pores in the sample, which can facilitate the ion migration, and (4) the interconnected architecture ensures good conductivity during electrochemical process. The detailed formation process and mechanism of the HPCNs are given in the Supporting information.

The HPCNs are constructed by Mg(OH)2 template induced assembly of the small coal-based GQDs. The diffraction peaks located at ca. 24 and 43° in the XRD patterns (Fig. 3 a) are identified as the characteristic peaks of carbons. Their broad and dispersive nature demonstrates that the HPCNs are of a low crystallinity. The HPCN-1: 3 shows the most intensive diffraction peak, thus the highest crystallinity of the three samples. The reason may be ascribe to that under the optimized ratio, the GQDs can densely and precisely cover the surface of the template, resulting in the formation of partially ordered carbon nanosheets. The peaks located at ca. 1350 and 1580 cm−1 in Raman spectra are the D and G band of carbons, respectively. The ratio of ID/IG is commonly used to represent its defect density [38–40]. Insufficient GQD precursor causes incompletely formation of carbon skeleton. So the HPCN-1: 4 shows the highest defect density and a relative low crystallinity (Fig. 3b). The peak at ca. 1100 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3c) is ascribed to the existence of COC. The peaks located at 284.6, 286.5, and 288.2 eV in the XPS C1s spectra (Fig. 3d) are corresponding to the CC, CO, and CO components in the samples, respectively [32,41,42]. The components of the HPCNs measured by XPS are seen in Table 1 . The precursor coal-based GQDs exhibit a high oxidative feature with enriched oxygen-bearing functional groups (Fig. S2). After carbonization at 800 °C, most of the oxygen-bearing functional groups are removed, while only small amount of COC and CO are remained. The HPCN-1: 3 and HPCN-1: 4 consists of ca. 93% of carbon and 7% of oxygen atoms. Excessive GQD precursor leads to a high oxygen content of 14 at. % of the HPCN-1: 2 (Table 1), which may be responsible for its low crystallinity. Though there is no direct evidence, we can speculate according to the experimental condition and the specific surface areas (Table 2 ) that the HPCN-1: 2 prepared using small amount of Mg(OH)2 template should perform a relative thick carbon layers. Its high oxygen retention may be due to the presence of oxygen inside its carbon skeleton rather than on the surface. We considered that the residual oxygen played an important role for the electrochemical energy storage of the HPCNs, which would be discussed later [43–46].

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms are plotted in Fig. 4 a, and the corresponding pore size distributions are given in Fig. 4b. The isotherms can be divided into three major processes occurring in the low (P/P0 < 0.1), middle (P/P0 = 0.1–0.8), and high (P/P0 > 0.8) relative pressure regions, corresponding to the presence of micro-, meso-, and macro-pores, respectively [43,44]. The HPCN-1: 2 shows a type II isotherm in where a rapid rising adsorption is observed at the high relative pressure region (P/P0 > 0.9), indicating that the pores are mainly dominated by open meso- and macro-pores (Fig. 4b). The HPCN-1: 3 and the HPCN-1: 4 show a type IV isotherm with quick adsorption in the low pressure region and a distinct hysteresis loop from the relative pressure of 0.4–0.99, indicating that these samples are of a hierarchical pore distribution consists of micro- and meso-pores. The HPCN-1: 3 shows the largest specific surface area of 1332 m2 g−1, micropore volume of 0.538 cm3 g−1, and well developed mesopore structure (Table 2). With the rising of template addition ratio, the total mesopore volume were greatly reduced. This can be ascribed to that under the optimized condition, the GQDs are fully and densely packed on the surface of appropriate amount of the Mg(OH)2 template. The gradually formed strong carbon networks during high temperature carbonization can prevent its hierarchical porous structure from coalescence and collapse. Excessive template may cause the incomplete interconnection of the GQD precursors. The relative weak carbon skeleton cannot prevent the pores from coalescence during carbonization. So the pore volume decreases by the partially coalescence of micro- and meso-pores.

The as-prepared HPCNs are of a three-dimensional interconnected networks constructed by two-dimensional thin-layer carbon nanosheets, resulting in their large specific surface areas, abundant active sites, and sufficient ion migration channels. Therefore, they are considered to be suitable for high performance supercapacitor electrodes [47–51]. Fig. 5 shows the electrochemical performances of the HPCNs prepared with different template addition ratio. The CV curves of the HPCNs under the scan rate of 10 mV s−1 (Fig. 5a) deviate from rectangular shape. And the charge–discharge curves (Fig. 5b) are slightly distort from triangular shape. These demonstrate that the energy storage mechanism of the HPCNs is dominated by both electric double layer and pseudocapacitive behaviors [52–56]. The conductive carbon scaffolds with large specific surface areas mainly contribute to the electric double layer capacitance. Meanwhile, the residual surface oxygen and the enriched defects or edges may play important roles for the pseudocapacitance [43,44,52–56]. The HPCN-1: 3 shows the best capacitive performance. The CV curves of the HPCN-1: 3 under different scan rates (Fig. 5c) indicates its fast charge–discharge ability because barely polarization is observed even under the high scan rate of 500 mV s−1. The CV curves of the other HPCNs are given in Fig. S7. From the galvanostatic test (Fig. 5d), the HPCN-1: 3 shows the specific capacitance of 200, 184, 177, 167, 145, 125, and 112 F g−1 under the current densities of 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 80, and 100 A g−1, respectively. Moreover, the HPCNs show excellent endurability, in where no obvious capacity fading is observed after cycled for 10000 times even under the current density of 10 A g−1 (Fig. 5e). EIS is taken to further evaluate the electrochemical kinetics of the HPCNs (Fig. 5f). The intersection points between the EIS curves and the real axis reveals the electric resistance of the electrode materials. The values of the HPCN-1: 2, HPCN-1: 3, and HPCN-1: 4 is 0.6323, 0.5588, and 0.5732 Ω, respectively. This reveals that the HPCN-1: 3 shows the best electric conductivity because of its high crystallinity and low defect density. Moreover, the radius of the semicircle in the high frequency region of the HPCN-1: 3 plot is smaller than those of the others. And the tails in the low frequency region of the Nyquist plot are nearly parallels towards the imaginary axis. These reveal that the HPCN-1: 3 shows a lower charge transfer resistance and higher ion migration rate than those of the other two. This is ascribed to that the suitable hierarchical porous structure allows good electric conductivity as well as facilitates the ion migration during electrochemical process. As a result, the best electrochemical performance of the HPCN-1: 3 is due to its unique hierarchical porous lamellar structure with a certain amount of surface oxygen, which provides (1) large specific surface area, (2) abundant active site on the surface, (3) good electric conductivity, and (4) sufficient ion migration channels. The HPCN-1: 2 exhibits the highest oxygen content (Table 1), but oxygen inside the carbon skeleton barely contribute to the capacitance. We also investigated the influence of carbonization temperature on the physicochemical properties and the electrochemical performances of the HPCNs by varying the temperature from 700 to 1000 °C. We find that the HPCN prepared at 800 °C showed the best energy storage performances though its specific surface is not the highest. The reason is possibly due to the balance between specific surface area, conductivity, and pseudocapacitance. The details are given in the Supporting information (Figs. S8–S12, Tables S1–S3).

To further improve the energy storage performance, in situ activation is performed by adding small amount of KOH in the precursors. The SEM and HRTEM images of the products with different KOH additions are given in Fig. 6 . Similar as the HPCNs, the AHPCNs still exhibit a thin layer loose-stacking structure. Some oxygen is still remained and uniformly distributed on the surface of AHPCN-1: 0.25 (Fig. 6c1–c3). In the AHPCN-1: 1, some small holes can be observed on the carbon sheets (Fig. 6e3) due to the severe etching by excess KOH.

The diffraction peaks of the AHPCNs in the XRD patterns are greatly weakened (Fig. 7 a), demonstrating their very low crystallinity. But from the Raman spectra given in Fig. 7b, the AHPCN-1: 0.25 and AHPCN-1: 0.5 show a lower defect density than HPCN-1: 3. Addition of KOH in the precursor disturbs the assembly of the GQDs to ordered structure during carbonization. On the other hand, acting as an activation agent, KOH can also etch the unstable carbon atoms at defect sites, thus reducing the defect density. The increased defect density of the AHPCN-1: 1 is due to the severe etching of the carbon skeleton by excessive KOH. The AHPCNs and the HPCNs show the similar FT-IR and XPS characteristics (Fig. 7c and d). From Table 3 , the oxygen content is similar, but the AHPCNs exhibit higher CO and CO component ratios than the HPCN-1: 3.

Fig. 9 shows the electrochemical performances of the AHPCNs. The AHPCNs perform similar CV (Fig. 9a) and galvanostatic charge–discharge curves as those of the HPCNs, also demonstrating their hybrid energy storage mechanisms. All of the AHPCNs show better capacitive behaviors and rate performances than those of the HPCNs (Fig. 9b–e) because of their larger specific surface areas, improved hierarchical porous structures, and more active surface sites (e. g. CO and CO components). The result indicates that our in situ activation is a simple and effective route to further improve the energy storage performance of the products even using very small amount of KOH. The CV curves of the other AHPCNs are given in Fig. S13. Though specific surface area of the AHPCN-1: 0.25 is not the highest, it performs the highest specific capacitance of 230, 210, 203, 195, 189, 175, and 170 F g−1 under the current densities of 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 80, and 100 A g−1, respectively. This is possibly due to its fine lamellar thickness and suitable porous distribution [57]. Electrochemical kinetics of electrode materials are dominated by their electric conductivity and ion migration ability. From the EIS results given in Fig. 9f, the radii of the semicircles in the high frequency region of the AHPCNs is smaller than that of the HPCN-1: 3, indicating the improved energy storage kinetics for AHPCNs. The AHPCN-1: 0.5 shows the best kinetics due to its low defect density and largest mesopore volume. From the discussion above, the improved electrochemical performance of the AHPCNs can be ascribed to (1) enlarged specific surface area and increased micropores provide more energy storage sites and (2) expansion of the mesopore volume provides more ion migration channels than those of the HPCNs.

We have developed a template-assisted assembly method to prepare the HPCNs using the coal-based GQDs as the building blocks. Under the optimized condition, the HPCN-1: 3 showed the specific capacitance of 200 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and a capacity retention of 112 F g−1 at 100 A g−1. To further improve their energy storage performance, in situ chemical activation was performed by adding only small amount of KOH during preparation. The AHPCN-1: 0.25 showed an improved capacity of 230 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 with a capacity retention of 170 F g−1 at 100 A g−1. All of the samples showed excellent cyclic stability with no obvious capacity fading even after cycled for 10000 times at 10 A g−1. Our work suggested that the coal-based GQDs are promising precursors for the preparation of advanced energy storage materials and may develop new ways for the clean and effective use of coal resource.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21471127 and 50572003) and the Fund of the State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering (No. CRE-2017-C-201).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.mtener.2017.08.003.
